NOTICE OF PLAINTIFFS MOTION TO VACATE DISMISSAL (TRANSACTION ID # 100072317) FILED BY PLAINTIFF SULLIVAN, JACQUELINE HEARING SET FOR JUN-20-2019 AT 09:30 AM IN DEPT 302

LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF JACQUELINE SULLIVAN'S MOTION TO VACATE DISMISSAL: PLAINTIFF JACQUELINE SULLIVAN'S MOTION TO VACATE DISMISSAL OF COMPLAINT IS GRANTED. PLAINTIFF HAS SHOWN THAT THE REQUEST FOR DISMISSAL OF THE COMPLAINT WITHOUT PREJUDICE WAS ENTERED AS A RESULT OF HER MISTAKE, INADVERTENCE, SURPRISE, OR EXCUSABLE NEGLECT, AND HAS TIMELY SOUGHT RELIEF. (CODE CIV. PROC. 473(B).) (SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR DETAILS) ORDER IS SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, CLERK: S. LE, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR#8600, MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM, REPORTED. =(302/EPS)

CRC 3.1385 ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Sep-23-2019 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jun-21-2019 Set For Order To Show Cause On Aug-13-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Plaintiffs Motion To Vacate Dismissal
Off Calendar Jun-20-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Thuy M. Le, esq. (415-398-5000) of Law Offices of Brian L. Larsen for Plaintiff.
No other appearances.

Not argued. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF JACQUELINE SULLIVAN's Motion To Vacate Dismissal:

Plaintiff Jacqueline Sullivan's motion to vacate dismissal of complaint is granted. Plaintiff has shown that the request for dismissal of the complaint without prejudice was entered as a result of her mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect, and has timely sought relief. (Code Civ. Proc. 473(b).)

Defendant mistakenly argues, relying on Huens v. Tatum (1997) 52 Cal.App.4th 259, that discretionary relief under Section 473 is not available for voluntary dismissals. Huens held only that "the mandatory 'attorney affidavit' provisions of section 473 cannot be used to set aside a voluntary dismissal executed pursuant to a settlement." (Id. at 265; see Zamora v. Clayborn Contracting Group, Inc. (2002) 28 Cal.4th 249, 256 ["the Huens majority confined its analysis to the mandatory relief provision of section 473, subdivision (b). Huens therefore has no bearing on our interpretation of the discretionary relief provision. Indeed, the same Court of Appeal has subsequently limited Huens to the mandatory relief context and applied the discretionary relief provision to a voluntary dismissal entered with the consent of the attorney for the party seeking relief."] (citing State Farm Fire & Casualty Co. v. Pietak (2001) 90 Cal.App.4th 600, 609-610).)

Moreover, in Zamora v. Clayborn Contracting Group, Inc. (2002) 28 Cal.4th 249, the Supreme Court disapproved Huens and expressly rejected the argument-identical to Defendants' position here-that Section 473(b) "applies only to involuntary judgments or dismissals." (Id. at 254, 256.) Rather, the Court observed, "California courts have long held that '[e]ven after a voluntary dismissal with prejudice has been filed, the trial court has jurisdiction to vacate the judgment of dismissal under Code of Civil Procedure section 473 where it has been entered as a result of the plaintiff's 'mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect.'" (Id. at 254-255, quoting Basinger v. Rogers & Wells (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d 16, 21.) Defendant's other arguments are meritless.

Order is signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
